astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-1.title: Order Earth, Mercury, Mars, and Venus by distance from the Sun.
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-1.problem_text: Order Earth, Mercury, Mars, and Venus by distance from the Sun.
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-1.solution: The order of the inner planets from the Sun is Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars.

astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-2.title: Why does the Sun, not Jupiter, determine the motion of the planets?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-2.problem_text: Why does the Sun, not Jupiter, determine the motion of the planets?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-2.given_data: the Sun is much more massive than the planets
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-2.solution:
Gravitational force is proportional to the mass of the attracting body.
The Sun contains almost all the mass of the Solar System, so its gravity determines the main planetary orbits.
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-2.answer: because of the Sun's enormous mass.

astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-3.title: How do terrestrial planets differ from gas giants?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-3.problem_text: How do terrestrial planets differ from gas giants?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-3.solution:
Terrestrial planets are smaller, denser, and have a solid surface.
Gas giants are more massive, consist mostly of hydrogen and helium, and have powerful atmospheres.
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-3.answer: terrestrial planets are rocky and dense; gas giants are large gas-liquid planets.

astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-4.title: Why is the asteroid belt not considered one planet?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-4.problem_text: Why is the asteroid belt not considered one planet?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-4.given_data: many small bodies between Mars and Jupiter
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-4.solution:
The belt consists of many separate bodies that did not merge into one planet.
Jupiter's gravity interfered with their stable accretion into a large body.
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-4.answer: it is not one body, but a region with many small objects.

astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-5.title: Why is studying small bodies important for understanding the history of the Solar System?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-5.problem_text: Why is studying small bodies important for understanding the history of the Solar System?
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-5.given_data: asteroids and comets are ancient objects
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-5.solution:
Small bodies often preserve material from the early Solar System.
Their composition helps reconstruct the conditions under which planets formed.
astronomy-solar-system-structure-task-5.answer: they contain information about early Solar-System material.

astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-1.title: A satellite moves on a circular orbit of radius 4R, where R is the radius of another orbit around the same planet. How many times smaller is its speed than on the orbit R?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-1.problem_text: A satellite moves on a circular orbit of radius 4R, where R is the radius of another orbit around the same planet. How many times smaller is its speed than on the orbit R?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-1.solution:
v is inversely proportional to √r.
v₂/v₁ = √(r₁/r₂) = √(1/4) = 1/2.

astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-2.title: For two planets orbiting the Sun, a₂ = 4a₁. How many times greater is the second planet's period?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-2.problem_text: For two planets orbiting the Sun, a₂ = 4a₁. How many times greater is the second planet's period?

astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-3.title: Why must a satellite in a low orbit move faster than a satellite in a high orbit?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-3.problem_text: Why must a satellite in a low orbit move faster than a satellite in a high orbit?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-3.solution:
At smaller r, gravitational attraction is stronger, so circular motion requires a greater centripetal speed.
The formula shows that as r decreases, speed increases.
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-3.answer: a smaller orbital radius requires a greater orbital speed.

astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-4.title: A planet has period 1 year at a = 1 AU. Find the period of a body with a = 9 AU around the Sun.
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-4.problem_text: A planet has period 1 year at a = 1 AU. Find the period of a body with a = 9 AU around the Sun.
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-4.given_data:
T₁ = 1 year
a₁ = 1 AU
a₂ = 9 AU

astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-5.title: Why does an orbiting satellite not fall vertically onto Earth?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-5.problem_text: Why does an orbiting satellite not fall vertically onto Earth?
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-5.given_data:
the satellite has horizontal speed
gravity is directed toward Earth
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-5.solution:
The satellite is constantly falling toward Earth under gravity.
But its horizontal speed is such that Earth's surface curves away beneath it, producing orbital motion.
astronomy-orbits-satellites-task-5.answer: the satellite is in continuous free fall around Earth.

astronomy-tides-task-1.title: Why does the Moon create stronger tides on Earth than the Sun, although the Sun is much more massive?
astronomy-tides-task-1.problem_text: Why does the Moon create stronger tides on Earth than the Sun, although the Sun is much more massive?
astronomy-tides-task-1.given_data:
the Moon is closer to Earth
tides depend on the nonuniformity of the gravitational field
astronomy-tides-task-1.solution:
Tidal force is related not to the total attraction, but to the difference in attraction between Earth's near and far sides.
Because the Moon is close, it creates a larger difference across Earth's diameter.
astronomy-tides-task-1.answer: the Moon is closer, so its tidal effect is stronger.

astronomy-tides-task-2.title: Why are tides usually higher at new moon and full moon?
astronomy-tides-task-2.problem_text: Why are tides usually higher at new moon and full moon?
astronomy-tides-task-2.given_data: the Sun, Earth, and Moon are nearly aligned
astronomy-tides-task-2.solution:
In these phases, lunar and solar tidal effects add together.
Therefore the tide amplitude becomes greater.
astronomy-tides-task-2.answer: the Moon's and Sun's tidal forces reinforce each other.

astronomy-tides-task-3.title: Why do tides occur not only on the side of Earth facing the Moon, but also on the opposite side?
astronomy-tides-task-3.problem_text: Why do tides occur not only on the side of Earth facing the Moon, but also on the opposite side?
astronomy-tides-task-3.given_data: the Moon attracts different parts of Earth unequally
astronomy-tides-task-3.solution:
The near side is attracted by the Moon more strongly than Earth's center.
The far side is attracted less strongly than the center, so relative to the center a second tidal bulge forms.
astronomy-tides-task-3.answer: because lunar attraction differs on different sides of Earth.

astronomy-tides-task-4.title: How does tidal friction affect the length of Earth's day?
astronomy-tides-task-4.problem_text: How does tidal friction affect the length of Earth's day?
astronomy-tides-task-4.given_data: tidal bulges interact with Earth's rotation
astronomy-tides-task-4.solution:
Tidal friction dissipates Earth's rotational energy.
Because of this, Earth's rotation gradually slows and the day becomes slightly longer.

astronomy-tides-task-5.title: Why do planetary satellites often have synchronous rotation?
astronomy-tides-task-5.problem_text: Why do planetary satellites often have synchronous rotation?
astronomy-tides-task-5.given_data: tidal forces act over a long time
astronomy-tides-task-5.solution:
Tidal deformations create torques that slow down or speed up the satellite's rotation.
Over time, the stable state is when the satellite always faces the planet with the same side.
astronomy-tides-task-5.answer: tidal forces synchronize the satellite's rotation with its orbital motion.

astronomy-eclipses-task-1.title: Determine the type of eclipse: Earth is between the Sun and the Moon, and the Moon enters Earth's shadow.
astronomy-eclipses-task-1.problem_text: Determine the type of eclipse: Earth is between the Sun and the Moon, and the Moon enters Earth's shadow.
astronomy-eclipses-task-1.given_data:
Sun - Earth - Moon
Moon in Earth's shadow
astronomy-eclipses-task-1.solution: When Earth blocks sunlight from reaching the Moon, a lunar eclipse occurs.

astronomy-eclipses-task-2.title: Determine the type of eclipse: the Moon is between the Sun and Earth and casts a shadow on Earth.
astronomy-eclipses-task-2.problem_text: Determine the type of eclipse: the Moon is between the Sun and Earth and casts a shadow on Earth.
astronomy-eclipses-task-2.given_data:
Sun - Moon - Earth
Moon's shadow on Earth
astronomy-eclipses-task-2.solution: When the Moon blocks the Sun for an observer on Earth, a solar eclipse occurs.

astronomy-eclipses-task-3.title: Why is a lunar eclipse visible from a larger area of Earth than a total solar eclipse?
astronomy-eclipses-task-3.problem_text: Why is a lunar eclipse visible from a larger area of Earth than a total solar eclipse?
astronomy-eclipses-task-3.given_data:
the Moon enters Earth's shadow
the Moon's shadow on Earth is narrow
astronomy-eclipses-task-3.solution:
A lunar eclipse is visible from the entire night side of Earth where the Moon is above the horizon.
The Moon's umbra on Earth forms a narrow strip.
astronomy-eclipses-task-3.answer: the visibility region of a lunar eclipse is much wider.

astronomy-eclipses-task-4.title: Why is it safe to watch a total lunar eclipse, but dangerous to look at the Sun without protection during a solar eclipse?
astronomy-eclipses-task-4.problem_text: Why is it safe to watch a total lunar eclipse, but dangerous to look at the Sun without protection during a solar eclipse?
astronomy-eclipses-task-4.given_data:
the Moon reflects weak light
the Sun is very bright
astronomy-eclipses-task-4.solution:
During a lunar eclipse, we observe the Moon, whose light is weak.
During a solar eclipse, even a partially uncovered Sun can damage the retina.
astronomy-eclipses-task-4.answer: the danger comes from direct solar radiation.

astronomy-eclipses-task-5.title: Why are eclipses possible only near the nodes of the Moon's orbit?
astronomy-eclipses-task-5.problem_text: Why are eclipses possible only near the nodes of the Moon's orbit?
astronomy-eclipses-task-5.given_data: the Moon's orbit is inclined to the ecliptic
astronomy-eclipses-task-5.solution:
For an eclipse, the Sun, Earth, and Moon must be almost on one line.
This is possible when the Moon is near the point where its orbit crosses the plane of Earth's orbit.
astronomy-eclipses-task-5.answer: near the nodes, the Moon crosses the plane where precise alignment is possible.

astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-1.title: During thermonuclear reactions, mass decreased by 1 kg. Estimate the released energy.
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-1.problem_text: During thermonuclear reactions, mass decreased by 1 kg. Estimate the released energy.

astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-2.title: Why cannot the Sun's energy be explained by ordinary chemical combustion?
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-2.problem_text: Why cannot the Sun's energy be explained by ordinary chemical combustion?
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-2.given_data:
the Sun shines for billions of years
the Sun's power is enormous
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-2.solution:
Chemical reactions release far less energy per unit mass than nuclear fusion.
Their energy reserve would not be enough for the observed duration of the Sun's luminosity.
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-2.answer: the Sun's energy source is thermonuclear fusion, not chemical combustion.

astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-3.title: Why are sunspots related to the magnetic field?
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-3.problem_text: Why are sunspots related to the magnetic field?
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-3.given_data:
spots are cooler than the photosphere
magnetic fields suppress convection
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-3.solution:
Strong magnetic fields interfere with the transport of hot material from deeper layers to the surface.
With less heat input, a region of the photosphere becomes cooler and looks darker.
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-3.answer: spots form in regions of strong magnetic field.

astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-4.title: Explain why solar flares are dangerous for satellites.
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-4.problem_text: Explain why solar flares are dangerous for satellites.
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-4.given_data:
solar flare
flows of particles and radiation
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-4.solution:
Flares and coronal mass ejections accelerate charged particles.
They can damage electronics, disrupt communication, and increase radiation exposure for satellites.
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-4.answer: because of charged particle flows and electromagnetic disturbances.

astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-5.title: The Sun lost 4×10⁹ kg of mass through emitted energy. How much energy corresponds to this mass?
astronomy-sun-structure-activity-task-5.problem_text: The Sun lost 4×10⁹ kg of mass through emitted energy. How much energy corresponds to this mass?

astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-1.topic_title: Stellar Spectra and the H-R Diagram
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-1.title: Two stars have emission maxima: the first in the blue region and the second in the red. Which star is hotter?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-1.problem_text: Two stars have emission maxima: the first in the blue region and the second in the red. Which star is hotter?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-1.given_data:
the first star is bluer
the second is redder
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-1.solution:
The higher the surface temperature, the shorter the wavelength of maximum emission.
Blue light has a shorter wavelength than red light.

astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-2.topic_title: Stellar Spectra and the H-R Diagram
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-2.title: Why can the chemical composition of a star be found from spectral lines?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-2.problem_text: Why can the chemical composition of a star be found from spectral lines?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-2.solution:
Each chemical element absorbs and emits light at characteristic wavelengths.
The set of lines in a spectrum is compared with laboratory spectra of elements.
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-2.answer: spectral lines are the fingerprints of elements.

astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-3.topic_title: Stellar Spectra and the H-R Diagram
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-3.title: A star has high luminosity and low surface temperature. In which region of the H-R diagram is it most likely located?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-3.problem_text: A star has high luminosity and low surface temperature. In which region of the H-R diagram is it most likely located?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-3.solution:
High luminosity at low temperature means a large radius.
Such stars are in the region of red giants or supergiants.

astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-4.topic_title: Stellar Spectra and the H-R Diagram
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-4.title: Why do white dwarfs have low luminosity despite high temperature?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-4.problem_text: Why do white dwarfs have low luminosity despite high temperature?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-4.given_data:
a white dwarf is hot
its radius is very small
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-4.solution:
Luminosity depends not only on temperature, but also on surface area.
White dwarfs have small radii, so their emitting area is small.
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-4.answer: because of their small radius and small surface area.

astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-5.topic_title: Stellar Spectra and the H-R Diagram
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-5.title: What does the Sun's position on the main sequence mean?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-5.problem_text: What does the Sun's position on the main sequence mean?
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-5.given_data: the Sun is a main-sequence star
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-5.solution:
On the main sequence, stars are in a stable phase of hydrogen fusion in the core.
The Sun is currently in this evolutionary phase.
astronomy-stellar-spectra-hr-task-5.answer: the Sun converts hydrogen into helium in its core.

astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-1.title: Why does a massive star live a shorter life even though it has more fuel?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-1.problem_text: Why does a massive star live a shorter life even though it has more fuel?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-1.solution:
Massive stars consume nuclear fuel much faster because of high core temperature and pressure.
The increase in luminosity outpaces the increase in fuel supply.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-1.answer: a massive star burns its fuel faster.

astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-2.title: Describe the evolutionary path of a Sun-like star after the main sequence.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-2.problem_text: Describe the evolutionary path of a Sun-like star after the main sequence.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-2.given_data:
solar-mass star
hydrogen in the core is exhausted
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-2.solution:
After the main sequence, the star expands into a red giant.
Then it sheds its outer layers, forming a planetary nebula, while the core becomes a white dwarf.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-2.answer: red giant → planetary nebula → white dwarf.

astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-3.title: Why are heavy elements formed and ejected in a supernova?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-3.problem_text: Why are heavy elements formed and ejected in a supernova?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-3.solution:
In massive stars, fusion reactions create heavy elements.
During the explosion, extreme temperatures and neutron fluxes allow even heavier nuclei to form.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-3.answer: supernovae are sources of heavy elements.

astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-4.title: Why does a star expand into a red giant after hydrogen is exhausted in its core?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-4.problem_text: Why does a star expand into a red giant after hydrogen is exhausted in its core?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-4.given_data:
hydrogen burning in the core weakens
shell burning continues
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-4.solution:
The core contracts and heats up, while the outer layers expand because the thermal balance changes and shell burning begins.
The surface cools, so the star becomes red.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-4.answer: reorganization of the internal energy source expands the envelope.

astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-5.title: Why does the final fate of a star depend on its mass?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-5.problem_text: Why does the final fate of a star depend on its mass?
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-5.given_data: mass affects core pressure and temperature
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-5.solution:
Greater mass causes stronger gravitational compression and allows heavier fusion stages.
Mass also determines what kind of remnant can resist gravity.
astronomy-stellar-evolution-types-task-5.answer: mass sets the thermonuclear stages and the type of compact remnant.

astronomy-compact-stars-task-1.topic_title: Neutron Stars, Pulsars, and Black Holes
astronomy-compact-stars-task-1.title: Estimate the Schwarzschild radius of a body with mass 6×10³⁰ kg. G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg², c = 3×10⁸ m/s.
astronomy-compact-stars-task-1.problem_text: Estimate the Schwarzschild radius of a body with mass 6×10³⁰ kg. G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg², c = 3×10⁸ m/s.

astronomy-compact-stars-task-2.topic_title: Neutron Stars, Pulsars, and Black Holes
astronomy-compact-stars-task-2.title: Why does a black hole not necessarily 'suck in' everything around it instantly?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-2.problem_text: Why does a black hole not necessarily 'suck in' everything around it instantly?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-2.given_data:
a black hole has mass and a gravitational field
distant objects can have orbits
astronomy-compact-stars-task-2.solution:
At large distances, the gravity of a black hole acts like the gravity of any body with the same mass.
If an object has enough sideways speed, it can orbit.
astronomy-compact-stars-task-2.answer: the region near the horizon is dangerous, but not everything nearby falls in instantly.

astronomy-compact-stars-task-3.topic_title: Neutron Stars, Pulsars, and Black Holes
astronomy-compact-stars-task-3.title: Why do pulsars produce periodic pulses?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-3.problem_text: Why do pulsars produce periodic pulses?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-3.given_data:
a neutron star rotates rapidly
radiation is emitted in beams
astronomy-compact-stars-task-3.solution:
The magnetic axis of a neutron star usually does not coincide with its rotation axis.
The radiation beam periodically points toward Earth, like a lighthouse beam.
astronomy-compact-stars-task-3.answer: because a directed radiation beam rotates.

astronomy-compact-stars-task-4.topic_title: Neutron Stars, Pulsars, and Black Holes
astronomy-compact-stars-task-4.title: The Schwarzschild radius is proportional to mass. If the mass of a black hole increases 3 times, how does R_s change?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-4.problem_text: The Schwarzschild radius is proportional to mass. If the mass of a black hole increases 3 times, how does R_s change?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-4.solution:
With constant G and c, the Schwarzschild radius is directly proportional to mass.
R₂/R₁ = 3.

astronomy-compact-stars-task-5.topic_title: Neutron Stars, Pulsars, and Black Holes
astronomy-compact-stars-task-5.title: Why do neutron stars have enormous density?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-5.problem_text: Why do neutron stars have enormous density?
astronomy-compact-stars-task-5.given_data:
remnant of a massive star
mass about solar
radius of tens of km
astronomy-compact-stars-task-5.solution:
After collapse, a large mass is compressed into a very small volume.
Most protons and electrons combine into neutrons, forming superdense matter.
astronomy-compact-stars-task-5.answer: a large mass is compressed to city-scale size.

astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-1.topic_title: The Milky Way and Galaxy Types
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-1.title: Why does the Milky Way appear in the sky as a bright band?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-1.problem_text: Why does the Milky Way appear in the sky as a bright band?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-1.given_data:
we are inside the galactic disk
there are many stars in the disk
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-1.solution:
When we look along the plane of the galactic disk, we see many distant stars and clouds.
Their combined light forms a bright band.
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-1.answer: it is an inside view of the stellar disk of the Milky Way.

astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-2.topic_title: The Milky Way and Galaxy Types
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-2.title: How does a spiral galaxy differ from an elliptical galaxy?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-2.problem_text: How does a spiral galaxy differ from an elliptical galaxy?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-2.solution:
Spiral galaxies have a disk, spiral arms, gas, and star-forming regions.
Elliptical galaxies are smoother, poorer in cold gas, and often contain old stars.
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-2.answer: spirals have a disk and arms; ellipticals have a smooth shape without a pronounced disk.

astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-3.topic_title: The Milky Way and Galaxy Types
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-3.title: Why can the motion of stars in a galaxy be used to judge the mass distribution?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-3.problem_text: Why can the motion of stars in a galaxy be used to judge the mass distribution?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-3.given_data: stellar motion is determined by gravity
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-3.solution:
Orbital speeds of stars depend on the gravitational field.
That field is created by all the mass of the galaxy, including invisible mass.
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-3.answer: stellar speeds show how much mass lies inside their orbits.

astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-4.topic_title: The Milky Way and Galaxy Types
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-4.title: Why does interstellar dust make it difficult to observe the center of the Milky Way in visible light?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-4.problem_text: Why does interstellar dust make it difficult to observe the center of the Milky Way in visible light?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-4.given_data: dust absorbs and scatters visible light
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-4.solution:
Dust clouds in the galactic disk weaken visible radiation from distant regions.
Therefore the Galactic center is studied better in infrared and radio wavelengths.
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-4.answer: dust blocks visible light from the center.

astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-5.topic_title: The Milky Way and Galaxy Types
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-5.title: Why can a galaxy not be considered just a random cloud of stars?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-5.problem_text: Why can a galaxy not be considered just a random cloud of stars?
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-5.given_data: stars move in a common gravitational field
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-5.solution:
A galaxy is a gravitationally bound system.
Stars, gas, dust, and dark matter form one dynamical structure.
astronomy-milky-way-galaxies-task-5.answer: a galaxy is held together by its own gravity.

astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-1.topic_title: Expansion of the Universe and Hubble's Law
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-1.title: A galaxy is 100 Mpc away. Take H₀ = 70 km/(s·Mpc). Find its recession speed.
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-1.problem_text: A galaxy is 100 Mpc away. Take H₀ = 70 km/(s·Mpc). Find its recession speed.

astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-2.topic_title: Expansion of the Universe and Hubble's Law
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-2.title: A galaxy has recession speed 14000 km/s. At H₀ = 70 km/(s·Mpc), estimate the distance.
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-2.problem_text: A galaxy has recession speed 14000 km/s. At H₀ = 70 km/(s·Mpc), estimate the distance.

astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-3.topic_title: Expansion of the Universe and Hubble's Law
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-3.title: A spectral line has wavelength 500 nm in the laboratory and is observed at 550 nm in a galaxy spectrum. Find z.
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-3.problem_text: A spectral line has wavelength 500 nm in the laboratory and is observed at 550 nm in a galaxy spectrum. Find z.

astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-4.topic_title: Expansion of the Universe and Hubble's Law
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-4.title: Why does the redshift of distant galaxies not mean they are flying through ordinary space away from one explosion center?
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-4.problem_text: Why does the redshift of distant galaxies not mean they are flying through ordinary space away from one explosion center?
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-4.solution:
In cosmology, space itself expands between distant galaxies.
There is no special center of expansion inside space from which everything flies away.
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-4.answer: redshift is related to the expansion of space, not to an explosion from one point in space.

astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-5.topic_title: Expansion of the Universe and Hubble's Law
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-5.title: If the distance to a galaxy is increased 3 times, how does the recession speed change according to Hubble's law?
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-5.problem_text: If the distance to a galaxy is increased 3 times, how does the recession speed change according to Hubble's law?
astronomy-hubble-expansion-task-5.solution:
Speed is directly proportional to distance.
v₂/v₁ = d₂/d₁ = 3.

astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-1.topic_title: The Big Bang and Cosmic Microwave Background
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-1.title: The temperature of the cosmic microwave background is about 2.7 K. How much is this above absolute zero in kelvins?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-1.problem_text: The temperature of the cosmic microwave background is about 2.7 K. How much is this above absolute zero in kelvins?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-1.solution:
The Kelvin scale starts at absolute zero.
A temperature of 2.7 K means it is 2.7 K above absolute zero.

astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-2.topic_title: The Big Bang and Cosmic Microwave Background
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-2.title: Why is the cosmic microwave background observed in all directions?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-2.problem_text: Why is the cosmic microwave background observed in all directions?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-2.solution:
The cosmic microwave background came from the hot early Universe, which filled all space.
Therefore we see it from every direction in the sky.
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-2.answer: it is radiation from all early space, not from one object.

astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-3.topic_title: The Big Bang and Cosmic Microwave Background
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-3.title: Why does the expansion of the Universe cool radiation?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-3.problem_text: Why does the expansion of the Universe cool radiation?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-3.solution:
As space expands, photon wavelengths increase.
Photon energy E = hc/λ decreases, so the radiation becomes colder.
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-3.answer: photons lose energy because their wavelengths are stretched.

astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-4.topic_title: The Big Bang and Cosmic Microwave Background
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-4.title: Why is the CMB considered strong evidence for the Big Bang model?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-4.problem_text: Why is the CMB considered strong evidence for the Big Bang model?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-4.given_data:
the model predicts a hot early Universe
nearly uniform microwave radiation is observed
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-4.solution:
A hot early Universe should leave thermal radiation behind.
Because of expansion, it cooled into the microwave range with a temperature of about 2.7 K.
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-4.answer: the CMB is the observed remnant of the hot early Universe.

astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-5.topic_title: The Big Bang and Cosmic Microwave Background
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-5.title: Why are small CMB fluctuations important for galaxy formation?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-5.problem_text: Why are small CMB fluctuations important for galaxy formation?
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-5.given_data:
the CMB has small inhomogeneities
gravity amplifies dense regions
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-5.solution:
Small regions of higher density attracted matter more strongly.
Over time, gravity amplified these inhomogeneities, forming galaxies and clusters.
astronomy-big-bang-cmb-task-5.answer: they became the initial seeds of cosmic structure.

astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-1.topic_title: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and General Relativity
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-1.title: Why do flat galaxy rotation curves indicate dark matter?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-1.problem_text: Why do flat galaxy rotation curves indicate dark matter?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-1.given_data: outer stars move faster than expected from visible mass
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-1.solution:
If only visible stars and gas are counted, outer orbital speeds should drop noticeably.
The observed motion requires additional invisible mass in the galaxy halo.
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-1.answer: dark matter is needed to create additional gravity.

astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-2.topic_title: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and General Relativity
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-2.title: Why is dark matter called 'dark'?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-2.given_data:
it produces gravity
it is not observed as ordinary luminous gas or stars
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-2.solution:
It does not emit or absorb light noticeably in ordinary observations.
Its presence is inferred from gravitational effects.
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-2.answer: it is electromagnetically invisible but has gravitational influence.

astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-3.topic_title: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and General Relativity
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-3.title: How does gravitational lensing help find invisible mass?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-3.problem_text: How does gravitational lensing help find invisible mass?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-3.given_data:
mass bends light
images of distant galaxies are distorted
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-3.solution:
From the amount of bending and stretching of images, the gravitational field can be reconstructed.
If there is more mass than visible stars show, this indicates dark matter.
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-3.answer: lensing shows the distribution of total mass, including invisible mass.

astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-4.topic_title: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and General Relativity
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-4.title: Why does accelerated expansion require a new component in the cosmological model?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-4.problem_text: Why does accelerated expansion require a new component in the cosmological model?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-4.given_data:
gravity of ordinary matter slows expansion
acceleration is observed
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-4.solution:
Ordinary and dark matter should slow expansion through gravity.
The observed acceleration is described by a component with a repulsive effect on cosmological scales.
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-4.answer: dark energy is needed to explain accelerated expansion.

astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-5.topic_title: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and General Relativity
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-5.title: How does general relativity's view of gravity differ qualitatively from Newton's?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-5.problem_text: How does general relativity's view of gravity differ qualitatively from Newton's?
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-5.solution:
In Newton's picture, gravity is a force between masses.
In general relativity, mass and energy curve spacetime, and bodies move according to its geometry.
astronomy-dark-cosmos-gr-task-5.answer: GR treats gravity as the geometry of spacetime.

astronomy-telescopes-types-task-1.title: Why can a telescope with a larger objective diameter see fainter objects?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-1.problem_text: Why can a telescope with a larger objective diameter see fainter objects?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-1.given_data:
larger objective diameter
larger light-collecting area
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-1.solution:
The amount of collected light is proportional to the area of the objective.
A larger objective collects more photons from a faint object.
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-1.answer: because of the larger light-collecting area.

astronomy-telescopes-types-task-2.title: The objective diameter was doubled. By how many times did the light-collecting area increase?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-2.problem_text: The objective diameter was doubled. By how many times did the light-collecting area increase?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-2.solution:
Area is proportional to the square of the diameter.
S₂/S₁ = (2D₁/D₁)² = 4.

astronomy-telescopes-types-task-3.title: Why are large professional optical telescopes often reflectors?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-3.problem_text: Why are large professional optical telescopes often reflectors?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-3.given_data:
large objective diameter
a mirror can be supported from behind
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-3.solution:
A large lens is difficult to make and deforms under its own weight.
A mirror can be made larger and supported from the back.
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-3.answer: large mirrors are technologically easier than large lenses.

astronomy-telescopes-types-task-4.title: Why do astronomers need telescopes in different ranges: radio, infrared, X-ray?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-4.problem_text: Why do astronomers need telescopes in different ranges: radio, infrared, X-ray?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-4.given_data: different processes emit in different ranges
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-4.solution:
Cold gas, dust, hot plasma, and compact objects are bright in different ranges.
One kind of light does not give a complete picture of an object.
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-4.answer: different ranges reveal different physical processes.

astronomy-telescopes-types-task-5.title: Why can a space telescope observe ultraviolet better than a ground-based telescope?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-5.problem_text: Why can a space telescope observe ultraviolet better than a ground-based telescope?
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-5.given_data: Earth's atmosphere absorbs most UV radiation
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-5.solution:
Ultraviolet radiation passes poorly through the atmosphere.
A telescope outside the atmosphere receives this radiation directly.
astronomy-telescopes-types-task-5.answer: the atmosphere does not interfere with a space telescope.

astronomy-angular-resolution-task-1.title: A telescope has D = 0.1 m and observes light with λ = 500 nm. Estimate θ ~ λ/D.
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-1.problem_text: A telescope has D = 0.1 m and observes light with λ = 500 nm. Estimate θ ~ λ/D.

astronomy-angular-resolution-task-2.title: The telescope diameter was increased from 1 m to 4 m at the same wavelength. By how many times did diffraction resolution improve?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-2.problem_text: The telescope diameter was increased from 1 m to 4 m at the same wavelength. By how many times did diffraction resolution improve?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-2.solution:
θ is inversely proportional to D.
If D increases 4 times, θ decreases 4 times.

astronomy-angular-resolution-task-3.title: Why do radio telescopes often have huge antennas?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-3.problem_text: Why do radio telescopes often have huge antennas?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-3.given_data:
radio waves have large λ
θ ~ λ/D
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-3.solution:
The larger the wavelength, the worse the resolution for the same diameter.
To compensate for large λ, D must be increased.
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-3.answer: a large diameter is needed for acceptable angular resolution.

astronomy-angular-resolution-task-4.title: Two objects are separated by 2×10⁻⁶ rad. A telescope has θ = 5×10⁻⁶ rad. Will it resolve them?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-4.problem_text: Two objects are separated by 2×10⁻⁶ rad. A telescope has θ = 5×10⁻⁶ rad. Will it resolve them?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-4.solution:
To resolve objects, the resolution must be smaller than or comparable to their angular separation.
Here θtel is larger than the separation.
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-4.answer: most likely no, the objects will merge.

astronomy-angular-resolution-task-5.title: Why can an image become larger but not more detailed at very high magnification?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-5.problem_text: Why can an image become larger but not more detailed at very high magnification?
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-5.given_data: details are limited by objective resolution and the atmosphere
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-5.solution:
Magnification stretches the image that has already been formed.
If the objective and atmosphere did not transmit fine details, magnification cannot create new information.
astronomy-angular-resolution-task-5.answer: magnification does not improve physical resolution.

astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-1.title: A hydrogen line is 656 nm in the laboratory and 656.3 nm in a star spectrum. Find the relative shift z.
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-1.problem_text: A hydrogen line is 656 nm in the laboratory and 656.3 nm in a star spectrum. Find the relative shift z.

astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-2.title: During an exoplanet transit, the star's brightness decreased by 1%. What does this mean qualitatively?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-2.problem_text: During an exoplanet transit, the star's brightness decreased by 1%. What does this mean qualitatively?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-2.solution:
The planet covered part of the star's disk for the observer.
The brightness drop is related to the ratio of the planet's area to the star's area.
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-2.answer: the planet covered about 1% of the visible area of the star.

astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-3.title: Why can spectroscopy measure a star's velocity along the line of sight?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-3.problem_text: Why can spectroscopy measure a star's velocity along the line of sight?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-3.solution:
Because of the Doppler effect, lines shift to the red when the source recedes and to the blue when it approaches.
The radial velocity is estimated from the magnitude of the shift.
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-3.answer: velocity is found from the Doppler shift of spectral lines.

astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-4.title: Why should photometric observations be compared with calibration stars?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-4.problem_text: Why should photometric observations be compared with calibration stars?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-4.given_data: the atmosphere and instruments distort the measured flux
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-4.solution:
Atmospheric transmission, detector sensitivity, and observing conditions change.
Calibration stars with known brightness allow measurements to be converted to a physically comparable scale.
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-4.answer: calibration removes the influence of the instrument and atmosphere.

astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-5.title: Strong calcium lines are visible in a star spectrum. Can we conclude that calcium is present in the star's atmosphere?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-5.problem_text: Strong calcium lines are visible in a star spectrum. Can we conclude that calcium is present in the star's atmosphere?
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-5.solution:
Spectral lines correspond to transitions of atoms or ions of a specific element.
If the lines are reliably identified, this indicates calcium is present.
astronomy-spectroscopy-photometry-task-5.answer: yes, the spectrum shows calcium in the atmosphere.

astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-1.title: A star has parallax 0.2 arcseconds. Find the distance in parsecs.
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-1.problem_text: A star has parallax 0.2 arcseconds. Find the distance in parsecs.

astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-2.title: A galaxy has z = 0.01. Estimate the distance for c = 300000 km/s and H₀ = 70 km/(s·Mpc).
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-2.problem_text: A galaxy has z = 0.01. Estimate the distance for c = 300000 km/s and H₀ = 70 km/(s·Mpc).

astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-3.title: Why does parallax work poorly for very distant galaxies?
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-3.problem_text: Why does parallax work poorly for very distant galaxies?
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-3.solution:
For distant objects, the angular displacement becomes too small to measure accurately.
Therefore other steps of the distance ladder are used.

astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-4.title: Why do radio observations help study the center of the Milky Way?
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-4.problem_text: Why do radio observations help study the center of the Milky Way?
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-4.given_data:
dust blocks visible light
radio waves pass through dust better
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-4.solution:
Interstellar dust strongly absorbs visible light.
Radio waves are scattered and absorbed less, so they reach us from hidden regions.
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-4.answer: radio waves allow us to see through dust clouds.

astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-5.title: Why are distances to type Ia supernovae used in cosmology?
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-5.problem_text: Why are distances to type Ia supernovae used in cosmology?
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-5.given_data: type Ia supernovae have similar standard luminosity after calibration
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-5.solution:
If the true luminosity of an object is known, its distance can be found from the observed brightness.
Type Ia supernovae are very bright, so they are visible at cosmological distances.
astronomy-distance-ladder-radio-task-5.answer: they serve as bright standard candles.

astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-1.title: Compare terrestrial planets and gas giants by composition and structure.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-1.problem_text: Compare terrestrial planets and gas giants by composition and structure.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-1.solution:
Terrestrial planets are rocky, dense, and have a solid surface.
Gas giants are much more massive, consist mainly of light gases, and do not have a familiar solid surface.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-1.answer: terrestrial planets are rocky; giants are gas-liquid and more massive.

astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-2.title: Why did Mars lose a significant part of its atmosphere more easily than Earth?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-2.problem_text: Why did Mars lose a significant part of its atmosphere more easily than Earth?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-2.given_data:
Mars is smaller than Earth
Mars's gravity is weaker
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-2.solution:
Lower mass gives a lower escape velocity.
Gas molecules can leave the planet more easily, especially under the action of the solar wind.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-2.answer: because of weaker gravity and less atmospheric protection.

astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-3.title: Why do gas giants have many satellites?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-3.problem_text: Why do gas giants have many satellites?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-3.given_data:
gas giants are massive
strong gravitational field
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-3.solution:
Large mass allows them to hold broad satellite systems.
Also, during formation, disks of material existed around giants, from which satellites could form.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-3.answer: because of their large mass and formation conditions.

astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-4.title: Why does Venus have a very strong greenhouse effect?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-4.problem_text: Why does Venus have a very strong greenhouse effect?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-4.given_data:
Venus has a dense atmosphere
lots of CO₂
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-4.solution:
Carbon dioxide traps infrared radiation from the surface effectively.
The dense atmosphere strengthens heat retention.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-4.answer: the dense CO₂ atmosphere traps thermal radiation.

astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-5.title: Why does Mercury have large temperature differences?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-5.problem_text: Why does Mercury have large temperature differences?
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-5.given_data:
Mercury is close to the Sun
almost no atmosphere
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-5.solution:
During the day, the surface heats strongly in sunlight.
At night, without a dense atmosphere, heat quickly escapes into space.
astronomy-terrestrial-gas-planets-task-5.answer: because of the weak atmosphere, which does not redistribute or retain heat.

astronomy-moon-satellites-task-1.title: Why do we see almost the same side of the Moon?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-1.problem_text: Why do we see almost the same side of the Moon?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-1.given_data: the Moon's rotation period equals its orbital period around Earth
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-1.solution:
With synchronous rotation, the Moon makes one rotation around its axis during one orbit around Earth.
Therefore the same side constantly faces Earth.

astronomy-moon-satellites-task-2.title: Why can astronauts' footprints remain on the Moon for a very long time?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-2.problem_text: Why can astronauts' footprints remain on the Moon for a very long time?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-2.given_data:
almost no atmosphere
no rain or wind
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-2.solution:
The Moon has no wind or water erosion like Earth.
Footprints can mainly be destroyed by micrometeorites and slow space weathering.
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-2.answer: because there is no normal atmosphere and weather erosion.

astronomy-moon-satellites-task-3.title: Why is Europa's subsurface ocean considered a promising place to search for life?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-3.problem_text: Why is Europa's subsurface ocean considered a promising place to search for life?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-3.given_data:
liquid water may exist
tidal heating is present
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-3.solution:
Liquid water is an important condition for known forms of life.
Tidal heating can keep the ocean liquid and provide energy sources.
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-3.answer: water and energy may exist under the icy crust.

astronomy-moon-satellites-task-4.title: Why does Titan retain a dense atmosphere better than the Moon?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-4.problem_text: Why does Titan retain a dense atmosphere better than the Moon?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-4.given_data:
Titan is larger than the Moon
low temperature
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-4.solution:
Stronger gravity helps retain gas.
Low temperature reduces molecular speeds, so molecules have more difficulty escaping the satellite.
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-4.answer: because of the combination of stronger gravity and low temperature.

astronomy-moon-satellites-task-5.title: Why are craters important for estimating the age of satellite surfaces?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-5.problem_text: Why are craters important for estimating the age of satellite surfaces?
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-5.solution:
The longer a surface has not been renewed by volcanism or erosion, the more impact craters remain on it.
Few craters often mean a younger or active surface.
astronomy-moon-satellites-task-5.answer: the number of craters helps judge surface age and activity.

astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-1.title: Why does a comet develop a tail when it approaches the Sun?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-1.problem_text: Why does a comet develop a tail when it approaches the Sun?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-1.given_data:
a comet contains ice and dust
the Sun heats the surface
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-1.solution:
Ices evaporate, carrying gas and dust around the nucleus.
Solar wind and light pressure push this material away from the Sun, forming a tail.
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-1.answer: the tail forms from evaporating ices and dust carried away from the Sun.

astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-2.title: Why is a comet tail not always directed backward along its trajectory?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-2.problem_text: Why is a comet tail not always directed backward along its trajectory?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-2.given_data: the tail is formed by solar wind and radiation pressure
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-2.solution:
The tail is determined not by the motion trail, but by the Sun's action on gas and dust.
Therefore it points mostly away from the Sun.
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-2.answer: the tail points away from the Sun, not necessarily backward along the orbit.

astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-3.title: During a transit, a star's brightness dropped by 0.01%. What does this mean qualitatively?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-3.problem_text: During a transit, a star's brightness dropped by 0.01%. What does this mean qualitatively?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-3.given_data:
a planet passes across the star's disk
brightness drop 0.01%
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-3.solution:
The planet covered a small fraction of the visible stellar disk.
The smaller the brightness drop, the smaller the ratio of the planet's area to the star's area.
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-3.answer: the planet covered a very small part of the star's disk.

astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-4.title: Why are asteroids important for estimating impact hazards to Earth?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-4.problem_text: Why are asteroids important for estimating impact hazards to Earth?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-4.solution:
If an asteroid's orbit crosses Earth's orbit, close approaches are possible.
Early detection allows the risk to be refined and protective measures to be prepared.
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-4.answer: near-Earth asteroids can pose an impact hazard.

astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-5.title: Why is direct imaging of exoplanets difficult?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-5.problem_text: Why is direct imaging of exoplanets difficult?
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-5.given_data:
a planet is much dimmer than its star
angular separation is small
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-5.solution:
The star's light is much brighter than the planet's light and overwhelms its image.
Also, the planet often appears very close to the star in the sky.
astronomy-small-bodies-exoplanets-task-5.answer: the planet is faint and located next to a very bright star.

astronomy-life-search-task-1.topic_title: Habitable Zone and the Search for Life
astronomy-life-search-task-1.title: Why is the habitable zone not the same as a zone where life definitely exists?
astronomy-life-search-task-1.problem_text: Why is the habitable zone not the same as a zone where life definitely exists?
astronomy-life-search-task-1.given_data:
the planet receives a suitable energy flux
other conditions are unknown
astronomy-life-search-task-1.solution:
The habitable zone mainly accounts for the possibility of liquid water based on temperature.
But atmosphere, planetary composition, climate stability, energy sources, and chemistry are also needed.
astronomy-life-search-task-1.answer: it is a zone of potential, not guaranteed, habitability.

astronomy-life-search-task-2.topic_title: Habitable Zone and the Search for Life
astronomy-life-search-task-2.title: For a brighter star, is the habitable zone farther or closer than for the Sun?
astronomy-life-search-task-2.problem_text: For a brighter star, is the habitable zone farther or closer than for the Sun?
astronomy-life-search-task-2.given_data:
the star is brighter than the Sun
liquid water requires moderate radiation flux
astronomy-life-search-task-2.solution:
If the star is brighter, a planet receives more energy at the same distance.
To have a moderate flux, the planet must be farther away.

astronomy-life-search-task-3.topic_title: Habitable Zone and the Search for Life
astronomy-life-search-task-3.title: Why can oxygen in an exoplanet atmosphere be a biosignature but not absolute proof of life?
astronomy-life-search-task-3.problem_text: Why can oxygen in an exoplanet atmosphere be a biosignature but not absolute proof of life?
astronomy-life-search-task-3.given_data:
oxygen can be produced by life
nonbiological mechanisms exist
astronomy-life-search-task-3.solution:
On Earth, oxygen is linked to photosynthesis, so it is interesting as a possible sign of life.
But some processes can create oxygen without life, so additional evidence is needed.
astronomy-life-search-task-3.answer: oxygen is important, but must be interpreted together with other data.

astronomy-life-search-task-4.topic_title: Habitable Zone and the Search for Life
astronomy-life-search-task-4.title: Why are red dwarfs interesting for exoplanet searches?
astronomy-life-search-task-4.problem_text: Why are red dwarfs interesting for exoplanet searches?
astronomy-life-search-task-4.given_data:
red dwarfs are numerous
small stars give noticeable transit signals
astronomy-life-search-task-4.solution:
There are very many such stars, so there are many potential planetary systems.
A planet blocks a larger fraction of a small stellar disk, making a transit easier to detect.
astronomy-life-search-task-4.answer: they are numerous, and their planets are easier to find by the transit method.

astronomy-life-search-task-5.topic_title: Habitable Zone and the Search for Life
astronomy-life-search-task-5.title: Why is not only water but also an energy source important for life?
astronomy-life-search-task-5.problem_text: Why is not only water but also an energy source important for life?
astronomy-life-search-task-5.solution:
Life must maintain complex chemical processes and internal order.
For this, it needs continuous access to energy: light, chemical reactions, or geothermal heat.
astronomy-life-search-task-5.answer: energy is needed to maintain metabolism and chemical activity.
